 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.3","title":"[TEST] Compile-all pack pattern validity (catch invalid regex without eager init)","description":"# Objective

Once patterns compile lazily, invalid regexes might only be discovered when a pattern is first used.

We want to preserve the strong property: "any invalid pattern fails CI immediately" **without** paying compilation cost on the runtime fast path.

# Approach

Add a unit test that:

1. Iterates all packs.
2. Iterates all safe + destructive patterns.
3. Compiles each exactly once.

This test is allowed to be slow-ish because it runs in CI/test, not in the hook fast path.

# Isomorphism

This test does not affect production behavior; it only enforces correctness.

# Acceptance criteria

- A single unit test fails if any pack pattern is invalid.
- Failure output identifies `pack_id` + `pattern_name` (and maybe pattern text).

# Notes

If/when patterns are migrated to `CompiledRegex`, this test should compile via the same abstraction so it also validates engine selection rules.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.4","title":"[TEST] Assert zero regex compilation on quick-reject allow path","description":"# Objective

Add a regression guard that proves we eliminated the primary startup cost:

- For a command that is allowed via quick reject (no enabled pack keywords), **no regex compilation should occur**.

This prevents future regressions where a refactor accidentally touches pack/pattern compilation on the allow fast path.

# Approach

- Add a `#[cfg(test)]` atomic counter inside the lazy regex compile path (increment when compiling).
- In a unit test:
  - Evaluate a representative allow command (e.g., `ls -la`) through the same code path used in hook mode (or as close as possible).
  - Assert the compile counter remains `0`.

# Important

This test must be careful not to compile patterns indirectly (e.g., by constructing `PackRegistry` in a way that eagerly builds patterns). It should reflect actual runtime behavior.

# Acceptance criteria

- Test fails if any regex compilation occurs on quick-reject allow commands.
- Test output clearly indicates the offending stage if the counter is non-zero (optional: include stage timing debug).

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","title":"[REFACTOR] Migrate SafePattern/DestructivePattern + macros to lazy regex","description":"# Objective

Stop compiling regex patterns during pack construction by migrating:

- `SafePattern`
- `DestructivePattern`
- pattern constructor macros (`pattern!`, `safe_pattern!`, `destructive!`, etc.)

…to store **pattern strings + LazyFancyRegex**, and only compile when matching.

# Scope

- Replace `regex: fancy_regex::Regex` fields with `regex: LazyFancyRegex` (or equivalent).
- Update all constructors/macros to build pattern structs without calling `Regex::new`.
- Update match sites to call `pattern.regex.is_match(...)` and `find_span(...)`.

# Isomorphism constraints

- Exact same pattern text.
- Same regex engine (`fancy_regex`) for now.
- Same match/first-match semantics.
- Same `pattern_name` propagation.

# Acceptance criteria

- `rg -n "Regex::new" src/packs` no longer shows pack pattern compilation at construction sites (except maybe special cases that will be handled in a follow-up task).
- `dcg packs --enabled` no longer spends ~175ms in regex compilation (verify after full integration).

# Tests

- Must pass existing unit tests and `./scripts/e2e_test.sh`.
- Must also satisfy new tests:
  - compile-all validity test
  - zero-compilation on quick reject allow path

# Notes

This task is a prerequisite for later:
- Swapping to `CompiledRegex` for linear patterns.
- Metadata-only registry (so listing packs doesn't instantiate pattern structs).

All 885 unit tests and 211 e2e tests pass.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:14:48.171539432-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:15:04.269430746-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:15:09.314336699-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:15:14.356525412-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:15:19.397765758-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.6","title":"[REFACTOR] PackRegistry metadata-only (no eager pack instantiation)","description":"# Objective

Refactor the pack registry so it can:

- list packs / keywords / enabled-pack ordering without instantiating full pack objects
- support hook quick reject without touching any regex machinery

…and only instantiate the pack structs/pattern objects needed for actual evaluation.

# Why this exists even after lazy patterns

Even if regex compilation is eliminated from startup, constructing *every pack* on every invocation can still be unnecessary work and can become significant as pack count grows.

Also, `dcg packs --enabled` should be metadata-only: it should never force full pack instantiation.

# Proposed approach

- Define static pack metadata (`PackSpec`) separately from runtime evaluation state.
- `PackRegistry` holds a slice of `&'static PackSpec` and provides:
  - `expand_enabled_ordered(...) -> Vec<PackId>` (deterministic)
  - `enabled_keywords(...) -> &[&'static str]` (or builds a union cheaply)
  - `get_pack(pack_id) -> Pack` (constructs runtime pack view on demand)

# Isomorphism constraints

- Deterministic pack ordering must stay identical to current behavior.
- Pack selection/tie-break for multiple matches must remain identical.
- No change in effective modes, allowlists, or fail-open semantics.

# Acceptance criteria

- `PackRegistry::new` (or equivalent init) does not allocate large vectors for packs/patterns by default.
- `dcg packs --enabled` runs without instantiating pack runtime objects.
- Hook allow fast path does not instantiate pack runtime objects.

# Tests

- Golden corpus unit tests (decision parity)
- E2E regression harness
- New regression: ensure pack ordering output is unchanged

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.7","title":"[PIPELINE] Hook fast path: parse input + keyword-gate before pack eval/compile","description":"# Objective

Reorder the hook-mode pipeline so we can exit quickly for allow-case commands without initializing/instantiating packs or compiling regex.

# Current problem

The current flow touches pack registry/keywords early, and registry init currently compiles regexes and/or instantiates packs. That makes trivial allow commands pay heavy costs.

# Desired flow (hook mode)

1. Load config (fast; baseline ~1.6ms for `dcg config`).
2. Read stdin JSON.
3. Extract the `command` string.
4. Compute enabled pack ids/order + keyword union using **metadata-only** registry.
5. Run pack-aware quick reject:
   - If no keywords found → allow (silent).
6. Only then instantiate packs / compile patterns / evaluate.

# Isomorphism constraints

- Must preserve existing semantics for:
  - heredoc handling
  - sanitizer/normalizer
  - allowlist overrides
  - mode selection (deny/warn/log)

This task is only changing ordering of *pure computations* and eliminating unnecessary work. All decisions must stay identical.

# Acceptance criteria

- `DCG_PACKS=...` + `ls -la` returns without regex compilation.
- `dcg packs --enabled` and hook allow path do not instantiate pack runtime objects.
- Measured allow-case p50 collapses from ~175ms to single-digit ms.

# Tests

- E2E suite passes.
- Golden corpus parity passes.
- New compile-counter test passes.
","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:16:26.797433357-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T16:16:26.797433357-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:16:26.798853283-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:16:37.177559315-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:16:42.219934048-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:16:47.260821049-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.8","title":"[PERF] Record before/after startup metrics + set budgets/guardrails","description":"# Objective

After implementing lazy registry + lazy compilation, re-measure and persist:

- p50/p95/p99 latency
- throughput
- max RSS

…for representative allow and deny cases, using the same harness as the baseline.

# What to measure (minimum)

Process-per-command runs (release build):

1. **Allow fast path**: command with no enabled keywords (e.g., `ls -la`).
2. **Typical keyword but allow**: e.g., `git status` (will still evaluate core pack).
3. **Typical deny**: e.g., `git reset --hard`.
4. **Worst-case packs enabled** (many packs via `DCG_PACKS=...`) but still allow fast path.

Also measure:
- `dcg packs --enabled` (should be metadata-only)

# How to measure (exact commands)

- Use the established harness from `git_safety_guard-ksk.1.3` (JSON output + build metadata).
- Use `/usr/bin/time -v` to capture max RSS.

# Success criteria

- Allow-case p50 collapses from ~175ms → single-digit ms.
- `dcg packs --enabled` collapses from ~177ms → single-digit ms.
- No decision diffs in golden/e2e harness.

# Output

- Store JSON results in a diffable location (in-repo) so CI can compare.
- Update perf budgets in `scripts/check_benchmark_budgets.sh` (or equivalent) to reflect the new reality.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.9","title":"[PERF] Reproducible startup profiling workflow (gprofng fallback)","description":"# Objective

Make the profiling step repeatable so future performance work can reliably identify hotspots **before** implementing changes.

# Why

Host environments may block `perf` (e.g., `perf_event_paranoid=4`). We already successfully used `gprofng` to profile dcg startup/pack listing.

This bead codifies:
- exact commands
- how to interpret outputs
- where to store artifacts

# Desired workflow

## CPU profile (startup-heavy subcommand)
- Use a loop to amplify cost and stabilize measurements.
- Prefer `dcg packs --enabled` as the reproducer for registry init.

Example (adapt as needed):
- `gprofng collect app -o /tmp/dcg_packs_loop_cpu.er -F '=dcg' -p on sh -c 'for i in $(seq 1 20); do ./target/debug/dcg packs --enabled >/dev/null; done'`
- `gprofng display text -functions /tmp/dcg_packs_loop_cpu.er | head -n 50`

## Heap profile
- `gprofng collect app -o /tmp/dcg_packs_heap.er -H on -p on ./target/debug/dcg packs --enabled >/dev/null`

## I/O sanity
If gprofng I/O init fails, use:
- `strace -c -f ./target/release/dcg packs --enabled >/dev/null`

# Deliverables

- A short doc snippet (in-repo or in a bead comment) that records:
  - profiling commands
  - common failure modes (perf blocked)
  - how to compare before/after
- Optional helper script under `scripts/` (only if it adds real value and doesn’t create duplication)

# Acceptance criteria

- Anyone can reproduce CPU hotspot identification in <5 minutes.
- Output clearly shows top functions by inclusive time.
","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- Run the profiling workflow on a release binary and capture commands, environment, and outputs as artifacts.
- Cross-check against perf harness baselines; record any deviations with logs.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:21:56.832215971-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T17:04:48.277679915-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:21:56.833710647-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mbq7","title":"[TASK] Documentation updates for new packs","description":"# Documentation Updates for New Packs

## Scope
Ensure all documentation is updated when new packs are added.

## Deliverables

### 1. Pack Listing in README
Update `README.md` with:
- Complete list of available packs by category
- Brief description of what each pack protects
- How to enable/disable specific packs

### 2. Pack Reference Documentation
Create `docs/packs/` directory with per-pack docs:
```
docs/packs/
├── README.md           # Index of all packs
├── secrets/
│   ├── vault.md       # Detailed Vault pack docs
│   ├── aws-secrets.md
│   └── ...
├── cicd/
│   └── ...
└── ...
```

Each pack doc includes:
- What commands are blocked (with examples)
- What commands are allowed (with examples)
- Configuration options
- Common false positive scenarios and how to handle

### 3. Configuration Guide
Create `docs/configuration.md`:
- How to enable/disable packs
- How to add custom allowlist entries
- How to configure pack behavior
- Environment variable reference

### 4. Troubleshooting Guide
Create `docs/troubleshooting.md`:
- Common issues and solutions
- How to debug false positives
- How to request new pack coverage
- How to report bugs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] README lists all packs
- [ ] Each pack has reference documentation
- [ ] Configuration guide is complete
- [ ] Troubleshooting guide covers common issues
- [ ] Docs build without errors","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- Add a doc consistency check (unit test or CI step) ensuring README pack list matches PackRegistry metadata.
- Run e2e harness to ensure documented examples still match actual output; log diffs if any.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:34:14.67283465-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T02:10:47.607531339-05:00"}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mcf","title":"Define JavaScript/Node destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define AST-aware matchers for JavaScript/Node heredoc bodies that detect **high-signal destructive operations** with minimal false positives.

## Scope boundary

- Focus on operations that are clearly destructive/irreversible.
- Do not turn this into a generic “JS security” scanner (avoid network/exfiltration heuristics).

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Recursive filesystem deletion (high signal)

- `fs.rmSync(path, { recursive: true, force: true })`
- `fs.rmSync(path, { recursive: true })`
- `fs.rmdirSync(path, { recursive: true })`

Severity guidance:

- `Error` only for clearly catastrophic literal targets (e.g., `"/"` or other policy-defined high-risk literals).
- Otherwise `Warning` by default.

### 2) Process execution with destructive payload (high signal)

- `child_process.execSync("rm -rf ...")` (literal)
- `spawnSync("rm", ["-rf", ...])` (literal)
- `exec("git reset --hard ...")` (literal)

Important:

- Do not flag `require('child_process')` alone.
- Do not flag `exec($VAR)` as a deny; it’s too common. At most warn if combined with other signals.

### 3) Known destructive package usage (optional / careful)

- `rimraf(...)` (common in builds) should likely be `Warning` unless literal catastrophic path.

## False positive mitigation

- Require negative fixtures that include common non-destructive uses:
  - `execSync("git status")`
  - `fs.rmSync("./dist", { recursive: true })` (should be warning at most)
  - code that merely prints dangerous strings

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines a JS/Node pattern set that is high-signal and severity-tagged.
- Includes at least:
  - 5 positive fixtures (must match)
  - 5 negative fixtures (must not match)
- No matcher becomes a default hard deny unless it is clearly catastrophic.
- Explicitly documents which constructs are intentionally not matched to preserve trust.
","notes":"CrimsonFalcon: taking over to align severity (catastrophic literal path blocks; otherwise warn) + add missing JS/Ruby positive/negative fixtures; will run full gates and push.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonFalcon","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:35:33.877595699-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T10:21:41.927767571-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T10:21:41.927767571-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented patterns + 5+ positive/negative test fixtures. All tests pass.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mcf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:35:39.990890431-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mcf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-f4f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:35:40.010899112-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mcf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-yza","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:51:25.768100887-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-me0","title":"[CATEGORY] messaging.* - Message Queue Pack Suite","description":"# Message Queue & Event Streaming Pack Suite

## Overview
This category covers protection for message queue and event streaming systems that are critical for modern distributed architectures. Accidental deletion of topics, queues, or consumer groups can cause catastrophic data loss and service outages.

## Packs to Implement

### messaging.kafka
- **Why Critical**: Kafka is the backbone of event-driven architectures at scale. Topic deletion, consumer group resets, or partition changes can cause permanent data loss.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `kafka-topics --delete` - Permanent topic deletion
  - `kafka-consumer-groups --delete` - Consumer group deletion loses offset tracking
  - `kafka-consumer-groups --reset-offsets` - Can cause message reprocessing or loss
  - `kafka-configs --alter --delete-config` - Config deletion
  - `kafka-acls --remove` - ACL removal
- **Safe Patterns**: `--describe`, `--list`, `--version`

### messaging.rabbitmq
- **Why Critical**: RabbitMQ manages critical async workflows. Queue/exchange deletion loses unprocessed messages.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `rabbitmqadmin delete queue` - Queue deletion
  - `rabbitmqadmin delete exchange` - Exchange deletion
  - `rabbitmqadmin purge queue` - Purges all messages
  - `rabbitmqctl delete_vhost` - Deletes entire virtual host
  - `rabbitmqctl forget_cluster_node` - Removes node from cluster
- **Safe Patterns**: `list`, `show`, `status`, `report`

### messaging.nats
- **Why Critical**: NATS/JetStream is growing in cloud-native deployments. Stream deletion loses historical messages.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `nats stream rm` / `nats stream delete` - Stream deletion
  - `nats stream purge` - Purges all messages
  - `nats consumer rm` - Consumer deletion
  - `nats kv del` - Key-value deletion
- **Safe Patterns**: `info`, `ls`, `list`, `view`, `report`

### messaging.sqs_sns
- **Why Critical**: AWS messaging services are deeply integrated into AWS workflows. Queue/topic deletion affects all subscribers.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `aws sqs delete-queue` - Queue deletion
  - `aws sqs purge-queue` - Purges all messages
  - `aws sns delete-topic` - Topic deletion
  - `aws sns unsubscribe` - Removes subscriptions
- **Safe Patterns**: `list-`, `get-`, `describe-`

## Rationale
Message queues are stateful infrastructure. Unlike stateless services that can be redeployed, deleted queues/topics lose their message history permanently. Consumer group offsets represent processing state that took time to build. This makes messaging systems high-priority for protection.

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 1 Epic (git_safety_guard-6ae)
- Blocked by: None (can start immediately)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All four packs implemented with comprehensive patterns
- [ ] Tests cover all destructive and safe patterns
- [ ] Documentation includes examples of each blocked command
- [ ] Integration tests verify pack loading and matching","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:27:16.875731243-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T14:27:16.875731243-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-kyi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:33:14.796224055-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-tlp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:33:14.826674296-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-aac","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:33:14.856192228-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:33:14.883589782-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:45.239517854-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:45.287980397-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:45.334762969-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:45.379658621-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:45.426034808-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-me0","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:45.473840816-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-me0s","title":"[AUDIT] Coverage Gap Analysis: Missing High-Impact Tools","description":"# Coverage Gap Analysis: Missing High-Impact Tools

## Purpose
Ensure we haven't missed any commonly-used tools that have destructive operations.

## Tools to Audit

### Build Tools (May need coverage)
- **npm/yarn/pnpm**: `npm unpublish`, `yarn remove`, package deletion
- **pip/poetry**: `pip uninstall`, registry operations
- **cargo**: `cargo yank`, registry operations
- **go**: module operations
- **maven/gradle**: repository operations

**Current coverage**: We have package_managers pack but unclear if these are covered.

### Container Registries (May need coverage)
- **Docker Hub**: `docker push` to wrong tag, tag deletion
- **ECR**: `aws ecr delete-repository`
- **GCR**: `gcloud container images delete`
- **ACR**: `az acr repository delete`

**Current coverage**: docker/podman packs exist but may not cover registry operations.

### Database Tools (Verify coverage)
- **psql**: Direct SQL execution
- **mysql client**: Direct SQL execution
- **mongosh**: Database/collection dropping

**Current coverage**: postgresql/mysql/mongodb packs exist. Verify CLI coverage.

### Log Management (May need coverage)
- **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**: We have search.* packs
- **Splunk**: Index deletion, forwarder operations
- **Datadog Logs**: Log deletion
- **CloudWatch Logs**: `aws logs delete-log-group`

**Current coverage**: Partial. May need additional packs.

### Secrets in Config (May need coverage)
- **.env files**: Accidental deletion/overwrite
- **config files**: Accidental credential exposure
- **git operations**: Committing secrets

**Current coverage**: git pack exists, but .env handling unclear.

### Infrastructure State (May need coverage)
- **Terraform state**: `terraform state rm`, state file deletion
- **Pulumi state**: Stack deletion
- **CloudFormation**: Stack deletion

**Current coverage**: terraform/pulumi packs exist. Verify state operations.

## Audit Process
For each tool category:
1. List all destructive operations
2. Check if existing pack covers them
3. If not covered, create new pack task or update existing
4. Prioritize based on usage frequency

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All tool categories audited
- [ ] Gaps identified and documented
- [ ] New pack tasks created for missing coverage
- [ ] Existing packs updated if patterns missing
- [ ] Priority assigned based on usage data","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- Produce a coverage matrix mapping existing packs -> unit tests -> e2e scenarios; identify gaps.
- Any newly proposed packs must include test/e2e hooks per Standard Pack Task Requirements.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:57:43.102378808-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T17:03:02.576667529-05:00"}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","title":"[PACK] messaging.sqs_sns - AWS SQS/SNS Pack","description":"# AWS SQS/SNS Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for AWS SQS and SNS operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### SQS
- `aws sqs delete-queue`
- `aws sqs purge-queue`
- `aws sqs delete-message`
- `aws sqs delete-message-batch`

### SNS
- `aws sns delete-topic`
- `aws sns unsubscribe`
- `aws sns remove-permission`
- `aws sns delete-platform-application`

## Safe Patterns
- `aws sqs list-queues`
- `aws sqs get-queue-attributes`
- `aws sqs receive-message`
- `aws sns list-topics`, `aws sns list-subscriptions`
- `aws sns get-topic-attributes`

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: messaging.* (git_safety_guard-me0)
- Keywords: aws, sqs, sns
- Note: purge-queue is immediate and irreversible

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Queue/topic deletion blocked
- [ ] Purge operations blocked
- [ ] Subscription operations handled
- [ ] Tests cover common SQS/SNS operations","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:31:56.205554046-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T14:31:56.205554046-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:32.575801648-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:32.617700601-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:32.658377004-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:32.697047193-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:32.735788786-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:32.77515319-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:09.220418757-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mkx","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:09.265510444-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","title":"[PACK] monitoring.newrelic - New Relic Pack","description":"# New Relic Monitoring Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for New Relic CLI and API operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### newrelic CLI
- `newrelic entity delete`
- `newrelic apm application delete`
- `newrelic workload delete`
- `newrelic synthetics delete`

### API/GraphQL
- GraphQL mutations with `delete` operations
- `curl` DELETE to api.newrelic.com

## Safe Patterns
- `newrelic entity search`
- `newrelic apm application get`
- `newrelic query` (NRQL queries)
- GraphQL queries (not mutations)

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: monitoring.* (git_safety_guard-adw)
- Keywords: newrelic
- Note: Uses GraphQL for many operations

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests for CLI delete patterns
- [ ] Unit tests for GraphQL mutation detection
- [ ] E2E test: `scripts/e2e_tests/monitoring_newrelic.txt`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Entity deletion blocked
- [ ] Application deletion blocked
- [ ] Query operations allowed","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:10:50.848759407-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T15:10:50.848759407-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:35.839581189-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:35.881953393-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:35.921986345-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:35.961542821-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:36.002775481-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:36.04360915-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:10.4030636-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-mp9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:10.451866015-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mvh","title":"Define Ruby destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define AST-aware matchers for destructive operations in Ruby heredoc bodies, focusing on high-signal runtime behavior.

## Scope boundary

- Focus on destructive filesystem/process operations.
- Avoid broad “metaprogramming danger” patterns (e.g., `eval`) as default denies; they are common and not necessarily destructive.

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Filesystem deletion (high signal)

- `FileUtils.rm_rf(path)` / `FileUtils.remove_dir(path, true)`
- `File.delete(path)` / `File.unlink(path)`

Severity guidance:

- `Error` only for catastrophic literal targets.
- Otherwise default `Warning`.

### 2) Process execution with destructive payload (high signal)

- backticks with literal destructive payload: `` `rm -rf ...` ``
- `system("rm -rf ...")`
- `exec("git reset --hard ...")`

Important:

- Do not flag `system($VAR)` as deny; too broad. At most warn if combined with other signals.

## Testing requirements

- Positive fixtures for:
  - `FileUtils.rm_rf("/")` (catastrophic)
  - `system("rm -rf /")`
- Negative fixtures for:
  - `FileUtils.rm_rf("./tmp")` (warning at most)
  - `puts "rm -rf"`

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines a Ruby pattern set aligned with severity taxonomy and focused on irreversible operations.
- Includes at least 5 positive and 5 negative fixtures.
- No default hard denies unless clearly catastrophic.
","notes":"CrimsonFalcon: taking over to align severity (catastrophic literal path blocks; otherwise warn) + add missing JS/Ruby positive/negative fixtures; will run full gates and push.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonFalcon","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:37:18.506057604-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T10:21:41.941491999-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T10:21:41.941491999-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented patterns + 5+ positive/negative test fixtures.
